mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes GLC220d or GLC250d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM651 2.1-litre diesel engine typically costs £4,000–£6,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £2,500–£4,500. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines can exceed £12,000.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes GLC220d or GLC250d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 is fundamentally reliable — 200,000–300,000 km examples are common. However, it has known weak points: timing chain stretch (the single most critical failure point, occurring from ~120,000 km / 75,000 miles), piezo injector failure (particularly on the 250d), DPF/EGR issues, and oil leaks. The 220d is generally more reliable than the 250d due to its use of more durable Delphi injectors.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes GLC220d and GLC250d?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start, misfires, cam/crank correlation codes), piezo injector failure (250d — rough running, vibrations, increased consumption), DPF clogging and EGR failures (urban driving), oil leaks from the timing chain tensioner seal (recall on 2014 models), and water pump leaks.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes GLC diesel engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £25,000 GLC250d, a £5,000 recon is 20% — worth it. Against a tired £10,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% Mercedes threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes GLC220d or GLC250d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. Both the GLC220d and GLC250d carry the engine code OM651. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — 'GLC 220 d' or 'GLC 250 d'. The OM651 is a 2.1-litre four-cylinder twin-turbo diesel engine used in the GLC X253 from 2015 to 2022.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain on the OM651, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain and tensioner replacement costs £800–£2,500. The OM651's timing chain drives both camshafts and the high-pressure fuel pump — if it stretches beyond tolerance or a guide fails, the consequences range from misfires to catastrophic engine damage if the chain jumps a tooth. The chain tensioner has been revised multiple times. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes using MB 229.51 spec oil are the single most effective preventative measure.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes GLC220d / GLC250d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the OM651 2.1-litre twin-turbo diesel engine. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLC diesel engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer).

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch & Tensioner Wear — All OM651
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the engine, engine warning light, misfires, cam/crank correlation codes, eventual catastrophic failure if ignored.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (chain + tensioner replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if chain snaps and valves strike pistons)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: Timing chain stretch is the single most critical failure point on the OM651. The timing chain drives both camshafts and the high-pressure fuel pump — if it stretches beyond tolerance or a guide fails, the consequences range from misfires and cam/crank correlation codes to catastrophic engine damage if the chain jumps a tooth. The chain tensioner has been revised multiple times, primarily affecting older OM651 engines. Over 100,000 vehicles with the OM651 engine were recalled in Germany for oil loss at the chain tensioner caused by manufacturing tolerances in the sealing rings. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain and tensioners inspected immediately. Regular oil changes using MB 229.51 spec oil are essential.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Piezo Injector Failure — Particularly 250d
problems.symptoms: Rough running, misfires, strong vibrations during acceleration, increased fuel consumption, engine warning light, poor cold start.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (injector replacement — up to four injectors)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if left untreated leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The 220 CDI and 250 CDI variants were equipped with Piezo injectors, which became known for their high susceptibility to failure. The 250 CDI never underwent the change to more reliable injectors, so even newer models may experience problems. The GLC250d is particularly vulnerable to injector issues. If you experience rough running or vibrations during acceleration, have the injectors tested. Using high-quality fuel can help extend injector life.

failureLink.label: Read the full fuel injector failure guide

problems.title: DPF Clogging & EGR System Failures
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, loss of power, increased fuel consumption, failed regeneration cycles, DPF warning light, hesitation under acceleration.
problems.repairCost: £300–£900 (DPF clean, EGR valve cleaning or replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if DPF/EGR issues lead to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The OM651's DPF and EGR systems are sensitive to short urban journeys. Soot builds inside the intake and EGR passages, causing inconsistent airflow. The engine's water-cooled EGR is integrated with the DPF system. If you predominantly drive in town, ensure the vehicle completes regular regeneration cycles — a motorway run at 2,000+ rpm for 20 minutes at least once a week is recommended. Using the correct low-ash oil is critical for DPF longevity.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF & EGR failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: GLC220d / GLC250d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Use quality fuel; catch it early
Regular regeneration and quality oil prevent issues
Cleaning or replacement — cheaper than engine replacement
Recall may cover it on affected models
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLC Diesel? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with evidence of regular oil changes using MB 229.51 spec oil
* Timing chain inspected or replaced (especially on higher-mileage examples)
* Injectors inspected (250d — piezo injectors are the main concern)
* DPF and EGR system regularly maintained (regular motorway runs)
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattle with no timing chain history
* Poor service history — the OM651 is a sophisticated engine that demands good maintenance
* Engine warning light with timing, injector, or DPF codes and no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The GLC220d and GLC250d are powered by Mercedes' OM651 twin-turbo diesel — a workhorse engine that served across the Mercedes range for over a decade. It's fundamentally reliable, with 200,000–300,000 km examples being common. However, it has known weak points: timing chain stretch (the single most critical failure point), piezo injector failure (particularly on the 250d), DPF/EGR issues, and oil leaks. The 220d is generally more reliable than the 250d due to its use of more durable injectors. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained GLC diesel is a £20,000–£35,000 premium SUV; a neglected one is a £5,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare GLC diesel engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes GLC220d or GLC250d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or metallic ticking (timing chain stretch warning)
* Service History — including which engine (220d or 250d) and confirmed oil-change intervals using MB 229.51 spec oil
* Timing Chain Check — Any history of timing chain or tensioner replacement? (essential on higher-mileage examples)
* Injector Check (250d) — Any rough running, misfires, or vibrations? (piezo injector warning)
* DPF/EGR Check — Any DPF warning lights or regeneration issues? (urban-driven vehicles are high risk)
* Oil Leak Check — Any evidence of oil leaks around the timing chain tensioner area? (recall on 2014 models)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, injector, DPF, or EGR issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
